title: References
text:
References

The following references are cited across this portfolio, formatted in APA 7th edition style.



Biggs, J., & Tang, C. (2011). Teaching for quality learning at university (4th ed.). Open University Press.


Boud, D., & Falchikov, N. (2006). Aligning assessment with long-term learning. Assessment & Evaluation in Higher Education, 31(4), 399–413. https://doi.org/10.1080/02602930600679050


CAST. (2024). Universal Design for Learning Guidelines version 3.0. https://udlguidelines.cast.org


Lucas, M., Bem-haja, P., Santos, S., Figueiredo, H., Ferreira Dias, M., & Amorim, M. (2022). Digital proficiency: Sorting real gaps from myths among higher education students. British Journal of Educational Technology, 53(6), 1885–1914. https://doi.org/10.1111/bjet.13220


Morgan, A., Sibson, R., & Jackson, D. (2022). Digital demand and digital deficit: Conceptualising digital literacy and gauging proficiency among higher education students. Journal of Higher Education Policy and Management, 44(3), 258–275. https://doi.org/10.1080/1360080X.2022.2030275


Sweller, J. (1988). Cognitive load during problem solving: Effects on learning. Cognitive Science, 12(2), 257–285. https://doi.org/10.1016/0364-0213(88)90023-7


Zhou, Y., Morgan, T., Sheridan, I., et al. (2025). Are Graduates Digitally Unprepared? A Digital Technology Gap Analysis From Alumni and Employer Perspectives. Journal of Computer Assisted Learning. https://doi.org/10.1111/jcal.70046


Zimmerman, B. J. (2002). Becoming a self-regulated learner: An overview. Theory Into Practice, 41(2), 64–70. https://doi.org/10.1207/s15430421tip4102_2

title: Qualifier 2: Facilitate a Wide Variety of Inclusive Learning Experiences for Students
text:
Qualifier 2 · Inclusive Learning
My approach to teaching and supporting student learning is centred on clarity, accessibility, and active engagement — particularly in areas that students often perceive as difficult or unfamiliar, such as bioinformatics and computational data analysis. I recognise that many biology students experience anxiety when encountering quantitative or coding-based content, and I deliberately structure my content to reduce these barriers while maintaining high-quailty teaching.

title: Qualifier 2: Facilitate a Wide Variety of Inclusive Learning Experiences for Students
section: Clear, Structured Explanations Linked to Biological Meaning
text:
Clear, Structured Explanations Linked to Biological Meaning
A key feature of my teaching is the use of clear, structured explanations supported by real-world examples in both lectures and practical classes. I aim to break down complex concepts into manageable components, while consistently linking each step back to the underlying biological question. This approach is particularly important in complex data driven subjects like CHEM320 and BIOL340, where students transition from experimental data generation to computational analysis.
During teaching sessions, I explicitly walk through analytical workflows, explaining not only what to do, but why each step is necessary and how it contributes to scientific interpretation. This reflects the principles of constructive alignment (Biggs & Tang, 2011), where teaching activities are explicitly designed to support students in achieving intended learning outcomes.
Student evaluation data consistently reflects the effectiveness of this approach. Across multiple subjects, I receive high scores for clarity of explanation, organisation, and subject knowledge (typically above 5.5/6.0), with particularly strong performance in bioinformatics teaching contexts. Students frequently comment that concepts are easy to understand and that the structured delivery helps them engage with complex material.


  
    
  

  
    BIOL340 (Autumn 2025, n=33)
    BIOL215 (Spring 2025, n=27)
    CHEM325 (Spring 2025, n=11)
    Max score = 6.0
  

  Note. Scores represent TEBS item means on a 6-point scale (1 = Strongly Disagree, 6 = Strongly Agree). Items selected reflect clarity of communication, organisation and explanation of coursework and assessments. Full evaluation reports are held in the evidence folder. BIOL984 Spring 2025 excluded (response threshold not met).





Figure 3: Student evaluation scores demonstrating high ratings for clarity, organisation, and explanation — see evaluation reports in the evidence folder.

title: Qualifier 2: Facilitate a Wide Variety of Inclusive Learning Experiences for Students
text:
Active Support and Interactive Engagement
Beyond clarity, I prioritise active support and engagement during learning. I adopt a highly interactive teaching style, regularly circulating during sessions, prompting discussion, and encouraging students to ask questions. I also integrate interactive tools such as Slido into lectures, using live polls and questions to encourage participation, check understanding in real time, and engage students who may be less likely to contribute verbally. This approach supports a range of learning preferences and aligns with Universal Design for Learning (UDL) guidelines by providing multiple means of engagement that appeal to different student interests and identities (CAST, 2024).

“Their lectures were very engaging, very clear information presented with lots of visuals to match the written information provided. Also employed a lot of audience participation and example exam questions, which made the lectures even more engaging and helped me pinpoint which pieces of information I was less sure on.” — BIOL340 student Autumn 2025
“Loved how thorough he went through his topic and made it easy to understand. Very interactive which kept me focused.” — CHEM325 student Spring 2025

title: Qualifier 2: Facilitate a Wide Variety of Inclusive Learning Experiences for Students
section: Separating Experimental and Analytical Components: Dedicated Dry Labs
text:
Separating Experimental and Analytical Components: Dedicated Dry Labs
A major development in my teaching practice has been the intentional separation of experimental and analytical components of learning. In BIOL340, I redesigned the practical sequence to introduce dedicated “dry labs” focused exclusively on data analysis and interpretation. This allows students to engage with experimental work and computational analysis as distinct cognitive tasks, reducing overload and improving conceptual understanding.
This approach is informed by cognitive load theory (Sweller, 1988): separating these components minimises extraneous cognitive load and allows students to focus on the underlying concepts and skills. It also creates space to explicitly teach data analysis as a core scientific capability, rather than treating it as an implicit component of laboratory work.

title: Qualifier 2: Facilitate a Wide Variety of Inclusive Learning Experiences for Students
text:
Block-Based Learning Activities
In response to student feedback that aspects of coding were difficult to understand, I introduced a series of block-based learning activities designed to shift the focus from syntax to conceptual understanding. In these activities, code is transformed into a set of physical “puzzle pieces” that students must organise into the correct analytical workflow. This enables students to engage with the logical structure of code without the additional cognitive demand of generating it independently.
This approach aligns with both cognitive load theory (reducing unnecessary complexity) and UDL principles (incorporating visual and tactile modes of learning alongside traditional instruction).






Block-based coding activity — physical puzzle pieces.







Online interface with modular code blocks.







Students working with block activities in class.





Figure 4: Block-based coding activity and corresponding online modular code interface.
This approach is reinforced through a custom-built teaching website, where code is presented in modular “blocks” that students can copy and use directly in their analyses. Together, these strategies emphasise that the key learning outcome is understanding the purpose and function of each step, rather than memorising or reproducing code — supporting a diverse student cohort by accommodating different levels of prior experience and preferred learning styles.

title: Qualifier 2: Facilitate a Wide Variety of Inclusive Learning Experiences for Students
section: Inclusive and Psychologically Safe Learning Environments
text:
Inclusive and Psychologically Safe Learning Environments
I intentionally design and facilitate learning environments that promote inclusivity, psychological safety, and active student participation. I explicitly normalise uncertainty and difficulty as part of the learning process, framing challenges as expected and manageable. This is reflected in student feedback indicating increased confidence in engaging with computational and data-driven content.
Student evaluation scores for respect, approachability, and encouragement of participation are consistently high (up to 6.0/6.0 in some cohorts).



Student evaluations — CHEM325 2024.





Student evaluations — BIOL215 2025.


Figure 5: Student evaluation scores and qualitative feedback highlighting supportive and engaging teaching environment.

Previous: Qualifier 1 — Curriculum Design · Next: Qualifier 3 — Learning Pathways

title: Qualifier 3: Support Students in Their Choice and Navigation of Formal and Informal Learning Pathways
text:
Qualifier 3 · Learning Pathways
I actively support students in navigating both formal and informal learning pathways by making learning processes explicit and providing structured opportunities for choice, progression, and self-directed development. A central principle of my curriculum design is that students should not only develop disciplinary knowledge, but should develop the ability to navigate their own learning — into the subject, through the degree, and into professional practice — aligning with the principles of Self-Regulated Learning (Bjork et al., 2013).

title: Qualifier 3: Support Students in Their Choice and Navigation of Formal and Informal Learning Pathways
section: Transition Into and Through the Curriculum
text:
Transition Into and Through the Curriculum
Many students arrive in BIOL340 from BIOL215 with strong laboratory foundations but limited experience in coding or data analysis. The early weeks bridge this gap through scaffolded practicals, curated resources, and flexible entry points designed to manage cognitive load Sweller (1988)] — introducing computational concepts incrementally so that new skills build on established disciplinary knowledge rather than competing with it. This scaffolded progression is embedded within a constructively aligned curriculum (Biggs & Tang, 2011), where learning activities, resources, and assessments are intentionally sequenced so that students can see a clear path from guided instruction to independent practice. I normalise this transition in my teaching language, framing new tools as an expected part of becoming a modern biologist. In lectures and dry lab discussions, I make connections across the degree explicit, highlighting how skills developed in BIOL215 and BIOL340 transfer into CHEM325 and honours research, helping students navigate their formal pathway as a coherent trajectory.

“They [the BIOL340 wet and dry practicals] worked very well together and had a good flow between them. They built knowledge from a more simple to a more complex level.” — BIOL340 student, 2025.

title: Qualifier 3: Support Students in Their Choice and Navigation of Formal and Informal Learning Pathways
text:
Student Choice and Informal Pathways
Within this structured framework, I create deliberate space for student agency. In the data visualisation components, students are provided with coding examples but encouraged to make their own decisions about how to present their data, using generative AI to explore alternative approaches that they then evaluate and justify. This aligns with UDL’s emphasis on providing multiple means of action and expression (CAST, 2024) — students engage with the same learning outcomes through pathways that reflect their developing confidence and creativity, rather than following a single prescribed route.
Beyond formal teaching, I have developed extension resources using Google NotebookLM, including podcasts and summary videos embedded in the Moodle site as informal learning tools. These provide multiple means of representation (CAST, 2024) allowing students to revisit concepts in formats that suit their learning preferences. Students are also encouraged to create their own study guides using similar tools, further developing their capacity for self-directed learning. The staged assessment structure supports this transition to independence: moving from a scientific report to a generative AI reflection task requires students to evaluate their own learning processes, promoting metacognitive awareness and reducing the cognitive demands of tackling both scientific communication and reflective thinking simultaneously (Sweller, 1988).


Video
Example lecture summary video





Your browser does not support the audio element. 

Google NotebookLM generated lecture summary podcast

title: Qualifier 3: Support Students in Their Choice and Navigation of Formal and Informal Learning Pathways
text:
Preparation for the Workforce
Digital literacy and by extension digital competence are essential skills in the post-Covid and post-Gen-AI eras (Zhou2025?). My curriculum redesign frames computational and digital literacy as essential biology graduate skills. In dry lab discussions, I situate what students are learning within the context of employer and research expectations. Student feedback supports this framing: BIOL340 students scored a mean of 4.75/6.0 on feeling equipped to work in Cell and Molecular Biology, with qualitative responses noting that computational activities reflect current industry practice.

Evidence: Student evaluation reports are available in the evidence folder. See also the BIOL340 teaching website.

Previous: Qualifier 2 — Inclusive Learning · Next: Qualifier 4 — Scaffolded Assessment

title: Qualifier 4: Design Scaffolded Assessments That Foster Progressive Learning
text:
Qualifier 4 · Scaffolded Assessment
My approach to assessment is grounded in the principle that assessment should both evaluate learning and actively support it. I design assessment tasks that reflect authentic scientific practice, requiring students to analyse data, interpret results, and communicate findings clearly. In subjects such as BIOL340 and CHEM325/BIOL984, this involves moving beyond recall-based tasks toward assessments that mirror real-world biological workflows — including data analysis, visualisation, and scientific writing.

title: Qualifier 4: Design Scaffolded Assessments That Foster Progressive Learning
text:
Constructive Alignment
Assessment design is guided by constructive alignment (Biggs & Tang, 2011), ensuring that tasks are directly linked to intended learning outcomes and supported by teaching activities. In BIOL340, this is exemplified through a staged assessment structure that aligns with the progression from experimental work to computational analysis and interpretation. Students are required to engage with authentic datasets and produce outputs that reflect real scientific practice, ensuring coherence between what is taught, what is practised, and what is assessed. Similar is explified in my resdesign of the CHEM325 bioinformatics assessment where students will engage with a real data set in class and their results that they build will form part of their scientific report assessment.

title: Qualifier 4: Design Scaffolded Assessments That Foster Progressive Learning
text:
The Multi-Stage Assessment Model
A key innovation in my assessment practice has been the development of a multi-stage assessment model, informed by my Learning and Teaching grant project for BIOL340 and aligned with institutional priorities around authentic assessment and academic integrity.
In this model, students first complete a series of practicals which are assessed through immediate post-laboratory quizzes which assess the immediate skills utilised in the practical. Following from this the student synthesise the results obtained across all practicals through a peer-reviewed scientific report, requiring them to formally analyse data and communicate findings in a scientific format. This then culminates in a generative AI reflection task, where students critically evaluate feedback on their report and engage with AI tools to assist in their revision and editing process (see CPD qualifier 2 for an example of the reflection task Pro Forma).
This staged design serves several pedagogical purposes:

Separation of skill domains — Separating the development of scientific communication skills from reflective and evaluative thinking reduces cognitive overload and allows students to focus on distinct learning objectives at each stage.
AI literacy and ethical awareness — Explicitly embedding AI literacy and ethical awareness into the curriculum ensures that students are not only using emerging tools, but critically engaging with their limitations and implications.
Iterative feedback — Creating opportunities for iterative feedback allows students to reflect on and improve their work across stages, rather than receiving feedback only at the end of the process.

These assessment are delivered via a customized feedback fruits workflow. Feedback fruits is an online interactive digital platform that navigates the student through submitting their assessment, providing feedback guided by a rubric, and then reflecting on the feedback the recieve.






Evidence: Example Feedback fruit workflow.

title: Qualifier 4: Design Scaffolded Assessments That Foster Progressive Learning
text:
Student Evaluation Data
We asked students in Biol340 evaluation data indicates strong performance in areas relating to clarity of assessment expectations, with mean scores typically above 5.5/6.0, suggesting that students understand what is required and how their work will be evaluated.
Furthermore, we separately surveyed students before and after the practicals and Generative AI embedded assessment and were able to utilise this data to determine improvements in student understanding of maintaining academic integrity while using AI, how to use tools effectively and the limitations of generative AI for scientific writing. See an excerpt of a presenation on AI and education delivered at ACSME 2025 demonstrating these results below (Figure 6). Importantly this assessment reform did not have negative affects on the overall performance of the students, instead leading to slight improvements in the cohort as a whole (Figure 6)




Additional evaluation data for assessment design.


Figure 6: Student evaluation scores relating to clarity of assessment expectations.

title: Qualifier 4: Design Scaffolded Assessments That Foster Progressive Learning
text:
Responding to Feedback
Evaluation data highlights feedback as an area for continued development, particularly in relation to timeliness and perceived usefulness (approximately 5.2–5.5/6.0). In response, I have implemented several changes:

Introducing formative checkpoints within practical sequences
Providing clear marking criteria and annotated exemplars
Refining email systems by utilising a subject and email and templates to more rapidly respond to student concerns.
Doing weekly check-ins via a moodle embedded student FAQ discussion board.
Increasing in-class, verbal feedback during practical sessions

These changes mean that feedback is integrated throughout the learning process rather than occurring solely at the end.






Evidence: Assessment design documents, marking rubrics, and the L&T grant application are available in the evidence folder.

title: Qualifier 4: Design Scaffolded Assessments That Foster Progressive Learning
section: Alignment with UOW Framework and Policy
text:
Alignment with UOW Framework and Policy
My teaching and curriculum development are grounded in UOW’s Standards and Quality Framework, Assessment and Feedback Policy, Subject Delivery Policy, and Code of Practice – Learning and Teaching. I approach these not as compliance requirements but as a practical scaffold for design decisions, using them to ensure that my choices serve both institutional standards and genuine student learning. Examples of the alignment between my teaching practice and UOW’s policy and educational frameworks are outlined below.







Practice
Framework Alignment




Constructive alignment of learning outcomes, activities, and assessment in BIOL340
Assessment and Feedback Policy 6: assessment tasks designed in accordance with Course Design Procedures and directly linked to subject and course learning outcomes.
Standards and Quality Framework Design Standard 7.4: learning activities, resources, and assessment are aligned to enable effective achievement of learning outcomes.


Multi-stage assessment model (peer-reviewed scientific report → GenAI reflection task)
Assessment and Feedback Policy 17-20: supports the ethical, transparent, and critically engaged use of GenAI in assessment.
Standards and Quality Framework Design Standard 10.1: criterion-based assessment appropriate to subject learning outcomes.


Explicit rubrics and detailed task descriptors
Assessment and Feedback Policy 8: explicit performance standards set out in a rubric made available to students when the task is set.
Subject Delivery Policy: assessment information consistent across all sources and communicated in the Subject Outline


Formative checkpoints, in-class verbal feedback, and staged workflows enabling action on feedback
Assessment and Feedback Policy 10-12: constructive and timely feedback; tasks scheduled to allow students to put feedback into practice.
Standards and Quality Framework Delivery Standard 9.2: teaching staff provide helpful and timely feedback on performance


UDL-informed design scaffolding diverse prior experience in coding and quantitative analysis
Assessment and Feedback Policy 22: assessment tasks designed to be accessible and inclusive in accordance with UDL principles.
Standards and Quality Framework Support Standard 9.2: UOW provides an inclusive teaching environment.
Code of Practice - Learning and Teaching 9 (teaching staff responsibilities): learning experiences offer variation and choice to support diverse cohorts and promote an inclusive learning environment.


Student evaluation data used iteratively to refine assessment design and feedback practice
Standards and Quality Framework Performance Standard 1: UOW collects and acts on student feedback to improve learning experience.




Previous: Qualifier 3 — Learning Pathways · Next: Qualifier 5 — Scholarship of T&L

title: Qualifier 7: Develop Effective, Resilient Professional Practices That Enhance Course Management
text:
Qualifier 7 · Professional Practice
My professional practice is characterised by a systematic and responsive approach to course management, underpinned by a commitment to continuous improvement, effective organisation, and adaptability in the face of evolving disciplinary and institutional demands.

title: Qualifier 7: Develop Effective, Resilient Professional Practices That Enhance Course Management
text:
Subject Coordination and Course Management
As Subject Coordinator for BIOL340 (Cell and Molecular Biology), I am responsible for the overall design, delivery, and administration of the subject, including:

Coordination of a multi-member teaching team
Management of practical sessions across wet and dry labs
Alignment of assessment with learning outcomes
Communication with students

The teaching team I coordinate comprises three academic staff with distinct disciplinary specialisations as well as a Technical Officer, who supports practical session delivery. Managing this team requires clear communication, proactive organisation, and the creation of shared materials and frameworks that enable each member to contribute effectively while maintaining a coherent student experience.
I also teach into CHEM325/BIOL984 (Bioinformatics) where I have also served as subject-coordinator and BIOL215, contributing to the management,coordination and delivery of subjects across both undergraduate and postgraduate levels.

Practical Demands of Coordination
BIOL340 involves a parallel sequence of wet and dry practical sessions, each requiring advance preparation of materials, briefing of teaching staff, and coordination of booking and equipment logistics with technical staff. I manage subject communication with students via Moodle announcements and email, handle queries relating to assessment and curriculum, and oversee academic administration including grade management and moderation.


Leading Curriculum Change
The substantial redesign of BIOL340 that I have led — transitioning from a quiz-only assessment model to a scaffolded, multi-stage structure incorporating wet labs, dry labs, peer review, scientific writing, and generative AI reflection — demonstrates my capacity to manage complex curriculum change while maintaining subject coherence and quality. This redesign required coordinating across multiple teaching and technical staff, developing new practical materials, and ensuring alignment with institutional standards and quality frameworks.
I am particularly proud of my technical support teams in Biol215 and Biol340 and have encouraged them to be co-developers of the practicals we deliver. These staff have used our innovations to enhance their own careers and professional practice and have been able to showcase their innovations, most recently through the SMAH L&T forum (Displayed below).

title: Qualifier 7: Develop Effective, Resilient Professional Practices That Enhance Course Management
section: Development of Teaching Resources and Infrastructure
text:
Development of Teaching Resources and Infrastructure
I have invested significantly in developing sustainable teaching resources that support both students and colleagues:

Custom-built teaching website for BIOL340, featuring modular code blocks, annotated workflows, and step-by-step guides that students can access independently
Annotated datasets designed to be reusable and adaptable across teaching sessions
Pre- and post-surveys to evaluate the impact of teaching innovations (such as the introduction of dry practicals), providing a structured mechanism for evidence-based reflection and continuous improvement

These resources reduce reliance on ad hoc instruction, improve consistency across teaching sessions, and support the scalability of teaching innovations — reflecting a professional approach to course management that prioritises sustainability and quality.

title: Qualifier 7: Develop Effective, Resilient Professional Practices That Enhance Course Management
text:
Resilience and Adaptability
A defining feature of my professional practice has been my capacity to respond constructively to significant changes in the higher education landscape, particularly the emergence of generative AI. Rather than viewing AI as a threat to assessment integrity alone, I have engaged proactively with the challenge:

Developing new assessment models that embed AI literacy
Contributing to school-wide policy through the SSCI AI Working Group
Sharing practical strategies with colleagues through workshops and presentations

This proactive, solutions-oriented approach reflects professional resilience and a commitment to turning disruption into opportunity for pedagogical improvement.






Evidence: Teaching coordination materials, teaching website, and pre/post survey instruments are available in the evidence folder.




Previous: Qualifier 6 — Evaluation & CPD · Back to: Home

title: Teaching Philosophy
text:
Teaching Philosophy
My teaching is grounded in the view that digital and computational literacy is essential for modern biology graduates. As biology becomes increasingly data-driven and professional workforces increase their expectations of graduate digital capabilities (Zhou et al., 2025), students must be able to interpret data, use computational tools effectively, and communicate findings clearly and ethically. These capabilities are central to developing critical thinking, problem solving, and communication skills. Indeed, the digital competency expected from employers has changed dramatically over last five years, as summarsied by Zhou et al. (2025) below, with COVID driving the uptake of hybrid working and collaborative digital workspaces and the recent integration of AI tools in many workplaces.

title: Teaching Philosophy
section: Embedding Computational Skills in Disciplinary Contexts
text:
Embedding Computational Skills in Disciplinary Contexts
I approach the teaching of computational skills by embedding them within disciplinary contexts rather than presenting them as abstract techniques. This reflects an interdisciplinary perspective that integrates biology with data science and supports students in applying knowledge across domains. It also recognises that confidence in the appropriate, effective and ethical use of quantitative and computational skills develops through supported, meaningful engagement with technology (Morgan et al., 2022).

title: Teaching Philosophy
text:
Active Participation and Authentic Learning
Active participation is a core principle of my teaching. I design learning experiences that mirror authentic scientific workflows, including data analysis, visualisation, interpretation, and writing. These are structured through constructive alignment, ensuring that learning outcomes, activities, and assessment are clearly connected (Biggs & Tang, 2011).

title: Teaching Philosophy
text:
Inclusive Learning Environments
I aim to create inclusive learning environments in which students feel supported to engage with challenging material and develop confidence in their abilities. This includes clear expectations, scaffolded learning, and opportunities for practice and feedback. This practice is informed by following the principles of Universal Design for Learning (CAST, 2024) and the concept of reducing cogntive load (Sweller, 1988)

title: Teaching Philosophy
text:
Summary
Overall, my teaching focuses on supporting students to become capable and adaptable graduates. By aligning learning with authentic disciplinary practice and providing structured support, I aim to enable deep engagement with biology and computer science while developing transferable skills for future study and professional practice.







Next: Explore how these principles are enacted across the seven CPD qualifiers.

title: Qualifier 6: Evaluate Teaching Practice, Support Teaching Teams, and Engage in Continuing Professional Development
text:
Qualifier 6 · Evaluation & CPD
I actively use evaluation data to reflect on and improve my teaching practice, and I contribute to the professional development of teaching teams within my school. My approach to evaluation is informed by a commitment to evidence-based reflection, where student feedback, peer input, and personal experience collectively shape iterative improvements to my teaching.

title: Qualifier 6: Evaluate Teaching Practice, Support Teaching Teams, and Engage in Continuing Professional Development
text:
Engaging with Student Evaluation Data
I regularly review and reflect on Teaching Evaluation (TEBS) data across the subjects I teach.
Combined evaluation data has consistently demonstrated strong performance, with an overall mean of 5.45/6.0 for 2025.
I engage critically with areas where evaluation data identifies opportunities for improvement. For example, feedback relating to timeliness of feedback and clarity of assessment expectations (approximately 5.17–5.27/6.0 in BIOL340) has informed specific changes to my practice, including the introduction of formative checkpoints, clearer marking criteria, restructured feedback workflows and a dedicated consulation meeting tool which facilitates students booking one-on-one meetings with me.
Qualitative feedback from students has also shaped my teaching: students have noted the value of interactive elements, the clarity of structured explanations, and the supportive learning environment, while also identifying areas where communication about assessment requirements could be strengthened. I use this feedback as a direct input into subject redesign and teaching refinement.






Evidence: Evaluation reports are available in the evidence folder.

title: Qualifier 6: Evaluate Teaching Practice, Support Teaching Teams, and Engage in Continuing Professional Development
text:
Supporting and Mentoring Teaching Teams
As Subject Coordinator for BIOL340, I am responsible for coordinating and supporting a teaching team that includes academic staff with diverse specialisations and a technical officer. Managing this team requires deliberate coordination, clear communication, and the creation of shared resources and frameworks that allow each member to contribute effectively to a coherent student experience.
I support team coherence through several mechanisms:

Pre-lab orientation materials — Prior to each practical session, I prepare and share detailed materials that set out the learning objectives, expected student background, and key technical and pedagogical points. These ensure all teaching staff can orient students consistently and address questions with confidence.
Pre-session briefings — I hold briefings with the teaching team to discuss session goals, anticipated student challenges, and any adjustments to materials based on feedback from previous cohorts.
Peer mentoring — I work alongside colleagues to share pedagogical strategies — for example, discussing how the scaffolded practical structure supports students with limited computational experience, or how the block-based coding activities can be facilitated to maximise engagement.

title: Qualifier 6: Evaluate Teaching Practice, Support Teaching Teams, and Engage in Continuing Professional Development
section: Supporting Colleagues Through the SSCI AI Working Group
text:
Supporting Colleagues Through the SSCI AI Working Group
I contribute to supporting colleagues through my active role in the SSCI AI Working Group, where I have helped establish school-wide guidelines for teaching with AI and designing resilient assessments. This work has involved leading workshops and delivering presentations to academic staff across the School of Science, sharing both conceptual frameworks and practical tools developed through my scholarship. Specific contributions include:

Presenting the GARI (Generative AI Risk Index) framework to school colleagues, providing a shared language and structured approach for evaluating and mitigating AI-related risks in assessment design.
Co-developing guidelines for the ethical and pedagogically sound integration of generative AI into teaching and assessment across the school.
Delivering workshops on AI in research and teaching contexts, supporting colleagues in developing their own approaches to AI-responsive pedagogy.


Impact Example: GARI Pilot Consultation
The practical impact of this mentoring is illustrated by one concrete example from the GARI pilot. Following a consultation arising from the working group’s assessment review, one subject coordinator reflected on a high-risk assessment and recognised that two of the GARI framework’s built-in mitigation strategies were applicable to their context: reducing the assessment’s weighting (thereby reducing the incentive for AI misuse) and providing explicit, specific guidance to students about permissible AI use.
By implementing these two targeted adjustments, the coordinator was able to reduce the assessment’s composite risk classification from “high” to “moderate” — without requiring a full redesign. This outcome illustrates how the GARI framework functions not only as an analytical tool, but as a practical scaffold that supports educators in taking evidence-based, proportionate action.






Evidence: SSCI AI Working Group presentation materials and evaluation reports are available in the evidence folder.

title: Qualifier 6: Evaluate Teaching Practice, Support Teaching Teams, and Engage in Continuing Professional Development
text:
Engaging in Continuing Professional Development
My engagement in CPD is driven by a recognition that effective teaching requires ongoing learning and adaptation. I have pursued professional development through multiple pathways:

Engagement with teaching and learning scholarship (detailed in Qualifier 5)
Attendance at institutional and external conferences
Collaborative work with colleagues on curriculum and assessment innovation
Sustained involvement in the SSCI AI Working Group
Completion of the 2024 UOW Early Career Academic Development Program

Indeed the creation of this portfolio itself has been a valuable opportunity to critically evaluate my teaching in relation to the CPD framework and identify areas for ongoing growth. Feedback from students, evaluation data, and the experience of scholarly engagement continue to shape my development as an educator.

Previous: Qualifier 5 — Scholarship of T&L · Next: Qualifier 7 — Professional Practice

title: Qualifier 5: Demonstrate Scholarship of Teaching and Learning
text:
Qualifier 5 · Scholarship of T&L
My engagement with the scholarship of teaching and learning is focused on the intersection of assessment design, generative AI in higher education, and the development of digital competency in biology graduates. I have pursued this scholarship through funded projects, conference presentations, collaborative research, and the development of practical frameworks that contribute to both institutional practice and the broader higher education sector.

title: Qualifier 5: Demonstrate Scholarship of Teaching and Learning
section: Learning and Teaching Grant: Redesigning Assessment in BIOL340
text:
Learning and Teaching Grant: Redesigning Assessment in BIOL340
In 2024-2025 I was awarded a UOW Learning and Teaching grant to support the redesign of assessment in BIOL340 (Cell and Molecular Biology). This project focused on transitioning the subject from a quiz-only assessment structure to an authentic, multi-stage model that integrates scientific report writing, peer review, and structured engagement with generative AI. The grant supported the development and evaluation of this approach, which has since become a central feature of the subject and informed my broader scholarship on AI-responsive assessment design.
The results of this grant and my curriculum redesign in Biol340 is also supported by an approved Human Ethics Application [H2025-1268]: Integrating Data Analysis and Computational Skills in Third-Year Cell and Molecular Biology and is currently being prepared for publication.

title: Qualifier 5: Demonstrate Scholarship of Teaching and Learning
text:
UOW Early Career Researcher Grant
How an AI Literacy Learning Module Shapes Students’ Understanding and Practices of Responsible Generative AI (GenAI) Use: A Qualitative Study – in progress (2026 – 2027)
This project aims to investigate whether intervention (e.g., AI learning module) will shape students’ ethical and responsible use of GenAI in learning. To do so, drawing upon the knowledge, attitudes and practices (KAP) framework, this study will examine students’ attitudinal and intentional change regarding GenAI use before and after an AI literacy learning module.

title: Qualifier 5: Demonstrate Scholarship of Teaching and Learning
section: The Generative AI Risk Index (GARI) Framework
text:
The Generative AI Risk Index (GARI) Framework
In collaboration with the School of Science (SSCI) AI Working Group, I co-developed a four-dimensional risk assessment framework for evaluating generative AI-related risks in higher education assessment.
The GARI framework conceptualises assessment risk as the balance between:

Exposure dimensions — task susceptibility and delivery mode
Mitigation dimensions — authenticity/personalisation and guidance/education

This work emerged from a systematic, school-wide analysis of all assessments conducted through the SSCI AI Working Group, involving both subject outline review and subject coordinator interviews. The framework was piloted across 2025 assessments within the School of Science, producing composite risk profiles that enable educators to identify vulnerabilities and target redesign efforts.
A scholarly paper is currently in preparation for submission to a peer-reviewed journal, framing the work within assessment validity theory, constructive alignment, and risk-based assurance approaches. The paper positions the GARI framework as a practical, educator-facing tool that addresses a significant gap in the literature — the absence of structured, operational tools for evaluating GenAI-related assessment risks in relation to learning outcome assurance.
I have also presented this framework at the university level through:

SSCI L&T planning days — to guide assessment redesign at the school level
SMAH faculty L&T forum
Sydney Basin STEM Education and Research Practice Symposium 2026 — as a workshop on Generative AI and assessment design, presented with collaborator Dr Jody Gorman

title: Qualifier 5: Demonstrate Scholarship of Teaching and Learning
text:
Conference Presentations and Dissemination
My teaching scholarship has been presented at multiple forums:

SMAH Learning and Teaching Forum

2024: Presented the multi-stage assessment model developed in BIOL340 and plans on how generative AI tools would be utilised to improve how students learn coding tools and self-assess writing.
2025: Presented “A Dimensional Risk Assessment of Generative AI in School of Science Assessments: A Framework for Transparent Evaluation”, sharing the GARI framework and its application.



Early Career Research Conference, UOW 2025
Delivered a workshop on “How to Utilise Generative AI to Enhance Scientific Writing Education”, sharing practical applications of AI to improve tertiary science education workflows with colleagues across disciplines.


Australian Conference on Science and Mathematics Education (ACSME) 2025
This presentation argued that rather than treating generative AI as a threat to be avoided, it should be embedded as a teachable graduate skill. The core thesis was that the modern cell biology graduate requires not only practical skills, data analysis, and core content knowledge, but also competence in using GenAI ethically — and that assessment should model and assure this.
The presentation reported on the redesign of BIOL340 assessment and its outcomes for student learning, AI literacy, and assessment integrity, framing GenAI in assessment as a “wicked problem” requiring nuanced, pedagogically sound responses.


Sydney Basin STEM Education and Research Practice Symposium 2026
Presented a workshop on assuring assessment integrity in the generative AI era, showcasing the Generative AI Risk Index (GARI) framework.

title: Qualifier 5: Demonstrate Scholarship of Teaching and Learning
text:
Summary
These presentations demonstrate my commitment to disseminating teaching innovations and contributing to scholarly conversations about teaching and learning in the context of generative AI. The progression from institutional grants through to conference presentation and manuscript preparation reflects an intentional trajectory toward sustained engagement with the scholarship of teaching and learning.






Evidence: Conference presentation slides, L&T grant documentation, and the GARI framework paper scaffold are available in the evidence folder.




Previous: Qualifier 4 — Scaffolded Assessment · Next: Qualifier 6 — Evaluation & CPD

title: Qualifier 1: Effectively design curriculum demonstrating integration across the degree
text:
Qualifier 1 · Curriculum Design
My approach to the design and planning of learning activities is grounded in the principle that modern biology education must develop not only conceptual understanding, but also the ability engage with technology to work with data, interpret results, and communicate findings effectively. As Subject Coordinator for BIOL340 (Cell and Molecular Biology), I have led a substantial redesign of the subject to align learning activities with authentic scientific practice whilst introducing digital literacy skills, ensuring that students engage with the kinds of workflows they will encounter beyond university.

title: Qualifier 1: Effectively design curriculum demonstrating integration across the degree
section: Integration Across the Degree: A Scaffolded Skills Progression
text:
Integration Across the Degree: A Scaffolded Skills Progression
My curriculum design decisions span three interconnected subjects that students typically encounter in sequence across their degree.

In BIOL215 (Introductory Genetics, Spring), students are introduced to foundational skills in genetics, genomics, and the analysis of sequence data. This provides the disciplinary and conceptual grounding for more advanced computational work.
In BIOL340 (Cell and Molecular Biology, Autumn), students build on this foundation through data analysis, visualisation, and interpretation using R, with computational skills now embedded within an experimental biology context.
In CHEM325/BIOL984 (Bioinformatics, Spring), the majority of the subject is dedicated to applying and extending these skills across a range of bioinformatics workflows — the most advanced treatment of computational biology in the undergraduate and postgraduate sequence.

This progression represents an intentional, degree-level design decision: rather than treating computational skills as isolated, subject-specific content, I have worked to ensure that each subject builds on and reinforces the skills developed in the previous one. Students arrive in BIOL340 with sequence analysis experience from BIOL215; they arrive in CHEM325/BIOL984 with data analysis experience from BIOL340. The result is a curriculum in which computational literacy develops progressively across the degree, culminating in advanced bioinformatics practice.
This alignment is consistent with the UOW Standards and Quality Framework for Learning, Teaching and Research Training, which emphasises that courses should be structured to provide coherence and progressive development of student capabilities.

title: Qualifier 1: Effectively design curriculum demonstrating integration across the degree
text:
Subject-Level Design: BIOL340
Prior to redesign, Biol340 was structured as a series of lectures, five wet labs each on a different topic and only quiz based assessments (practical and theory).
Central to this redesign is the concept of constructive alignment (Biggs & Tang, 2011), where learning outcomes, teaching activities, and assessment are intentionally integrated. In BIOL340, this is achieved through a scaffolded sequence of wet and dry practicals that mirror a complete research pipeline culminating in two assessments, 1) scientific report and 2) a reflection task. Students begin with experimental data generation (e.g., cell viability assays), and then transition into data analysis, visualisation, and interpretation using R. This structure ensures that computational skills are not taught in isolation, but are embedded within meaningful biological contexts.


Figure 1: Overview of BIOL340 practical sequence showing alignment between wet lab, dry lab, and assessment tasks.

Scaffolding for Diverse Student Backgrounds
A key design consideration has been scaffolding for diverse student backgrounds, particularly in computational skills. Recognising that many students enter with limited experience in coding or quantitative analysis, I have structured learning activities to progressively build competence. Early tasks provide highly guided, step-by-step instruction, while later activities require increasing independence in data interpretation and problem-solving. This approach is informed by cognitive load theory (Sweller, 1988), reducing extraneous load while maintaining appropriate academic challenge.
To support this, I have developed a suite of custom teaching materials, including a beginner-friendly website, practicals, annotated datasets, and structured workflows that explicitly guide students through the process of importing, cleaning, analysing, and visualising data. These materials are designed to be both instructional and reusable, allowing students to revisit concepts as needed. Importantly, the dry practicals are designed such that students have capacity to learn the logic of data science without cognitive overload from learning to code.

Figure 2: Example of Quarto practical showing scaffolded data analysis workflow. View dry practical 1 website

title: Qualifier 1: Effectively design curriculum demonstrating integration across the degree
text:
Integration into CHEM325/BIOL984
This design philosophy extends beyond BIOL340 into my teaching in bioinformatics (CHEM325/BIOL984), where I similarly emphasise the integration of theory with applied, real-world datasets. I have redesigned assessments in CHEM325 to equip students with the skills to analyse large-scale real-world datasets, including a formal report that explores the evolution and epidemiology of Zika virus on data personalised for each student (through a custom script). I similarly apply principles of constructive alignment where computer practicals allow students to obtain and analyse their data, which then directly translates into the results section of their formal report.
Student feedback consistently highlights the value of this approach, with mean scores of 5.8/6.0 for connecting theory with practical applications — indicating that students recognise the relevance and coherence of the learning activities.

title: Qualifier 1: Effectively design curriculum demonstrating integration across the degree
text:
Iterative Improvement
Importantly, the design of learning activities is informed by ongoing reflection and evaluation. Feedback from BIOL340 identified that while students valued the authenticity of computational tasks, some experienced difficulty when transitioning to independent analysis. In response, I have refined activity design to include clearer transitions between guided and independent work, additional explanatory material, and more explicit links between each analytical step and its biological meaning. This iterative approach reflects a commitment to continuous improvement aligned with the UOW Standards and Quality Framework for Learning, Teaching and Research Training.






Evidence: See also the BIOL340 dry practical website. Subject evaluation data is available

title: Teaching Philosophy
text:
Assessment and Feedback
Assessment is designed to be authentic and to require application, justification, and clear communication. I incorporate opportunities for feedback and reflection, supporting the development of self-regulated learners. Where appropriate, I integrate generative AI in a structured way, requiring students to evaluate and refine outputs. This supports ethical engagement with emerging technologies and aligns with contemporary approaches to assessment and feedback.

title: Qualifier 2: Facilitate a Wide Variety of Inclusive Learning Experiences for Students
section: Separating Experimental and Analytical Components: Reducing Cognitive Load
text:
Separating Experimental and Analytical Components: Reducing Cognitive Load
A major development in my teaching practice has been the intentional separation of experimental and analytical components of learning. In BIOL340, I redesigned the practical sequence to introduce dedicated “dry labs” focused exclusively on data analysis and interpretation. This allows students to engage with experimental work and computational analysis as distinct cognitive tasks, reducing overload and improving conceptual understanding.
This approach is informed by cognitive load theory (Sweller, 1988): separating these components minimises extraneous cognitive load and allows students to focus on the underlying concepts and skills. It also creates space to explicitly teach data analysis as a core scientific capability, rather than treating it as an implicit component of laboratory work.
This idea of reducing cognitive load to improve learning outcomes extends into how I develop my assessments. In BIOL340 I utilise scaffolded approach (outlined in detail in CPD descriptor 4) that reduces student mental load by separates the processes of writing, editing and reflecting. In the final reflection phase students are asked to use a previous piece of their writing as a basis for a guided workshop on how AI tools can be used ethically to improve scientific writing. This process is aided through a Pro Forma (see below) which outlines key prompting strategies and itself acts as a journal of their editing and reflection process.

title: Qualifier 4: Design Scaffolded Assessments That Foster Progressive Learning
section: Theoretical basis: Constructive Alignment and Cognitive load theory
text:
Theoretical basis: Constructive Alignment and Cognitive load theory
My assessment design is guided by a combination of Constructive Alignment (Biggs & Tang, 2011), ensuring that tasks are directly linked to intended learning outcomes and supported by teaching activities and Cognitive Load Theory (Sweller, 1988), whereby discrete types of learning are separated to reduce cognitive overload and promote better student outcomes. In BIOL340, this is exemplified through a staged assessment structure that aligns with the progression from experimental work to computational analysis and interpretation. Students are required to engage with authentic datasets and produce outputs that reflect real scientific practice, ensuring coherence between what is taught, what is practiced, and what is assessed. Similar is exemplified in my redesign of the CHEM325 bioinformatics assessment where students will engage with a real data set in class, and in-turn their results form the basis of their scientific report assessment.
